attributes.stem:
A review is requested for a 50-year-old man on day four of hospital admission for acute diverticulitis with reduced urine output. There has been no urine output over the past 12 hours. He has an indwelling urinary catheter which has been flushed multiple times. Six hours ago he was commenced on antibiotics for worsening symptoms. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 15/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. His mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced. His bladder is not palpable. There is tenderness in the left lower quadrant of his abdomen. Blood tests from two days ago, including complete blood examination and biochemistry, were within normal limits. Today they show:

  • Haemoglobin 140 g/L (115-155)

  • White cell count 9  x109 /L (4.0-10.0)

  • Creatinine 150 umol/L (53-115).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of his acute kidney injury?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has developed an acute kidney injury (AKI) with oliguria and a significant rise in creatinine from baseline. The clinical features strongly suggest hypovolaemia as the cause, with tachycardia, hypotension, dry mucous membranes, and reduced skin turgor. Pre-renal AKI due to hypovolaemia is the most common cause of AKI in hospitalised patients.

Nephrotoxicity from medications, particularly antibiotics, is an important cause of hospital-acquired AKI. However, in this case the oliguria developed before antibiotic administration, making this unlikely to be the primary cause.

Medication side effects affecting micturition typically cause urinary retention rather than oliguria. The presence of an indwelling catheter and non-palpable bladder makes this unlikely.

Glomerulonephritis would be considered if pre-renal and post-renal causes were excluded. There are no typical features such as haematuria, proteinuria, or systemic symptoms to suggest this diagnosis.

Worsening diverticulitis alone would not explain the AKI. While infection and inflammation can contribute to kidney injury, the normal white cell count and absence of systemic inflammatory response make this unlikely to be the primary cause.

The management priority is fluid resuscitation with close monitoring of fluid balance and repeat assessment of renal function. If the patient does not respond to fluid resuscitation, further investigation for other causes would be warranted.
learningPoints: Acute kidney injury is defined by a creatinine rise ≥26.5 umol/L in 48 hours or oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hour for 6 hours.
Clinical assessment of volume status is critical in evaluating acute kidney injury.
Pre-renal causes (hypovolaemia) and post-renal causes (obstruction) must be excluded before investigating intrinsic renal causes.
references: Ostermann, M., Joannidis, M. Acute kidney injury 2016: diagnosis and diagnostic workup. Crit Care. 2016 Sep:20(1);299. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1186/s13054-016-1478-z

Morales-Alvarez MC. Nephrotoxicity of antimicrobials and antibiotics. Adv Chronic Kidney Dis. 2020 Jan;27(1):31–37. doi: 10.1053/j.ackd.2019.08.001. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1053/j.ackd.2019.08.001

Nadasdy T, Lee AH. Infection-related glomerulonephritis: understanding mechanisms. Semin Nephrol. 2011 Jul;31(4):369–375. doi: 10.1016/j.semnephrol.2011.06.008. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.semnephrol.2011.06.008
